value.Title: Trump spurs billion-dollar cuts to Labor's green hydrogen dream
value.Overview: The Age (Melbourne, Australia) - Online, 822words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK2-B373-RS4R-915B-00000-00Trump spurs billion-dollar cuts to Labor's green hydrogen dream2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisTrump spurs billion-dollar cuts to Labor's green hydrogen dreamMike Foley

The federal budget has revealed a major change to government's goals to revitalise local manufacturing.

Labor has slashed billions of dollars from its ambitions for hydrogen exports and a flourishing clean manufacturing sector as the Trump administration's anti-clean-energy agenda hits global investment and cripples the Albanese government's green industry goals.

This week's federal budget removed $1 billion from the program that the government said would turn Australia into a global powerhouse of green hydrogen, an energy source that burns cleanly and could replace fossil fuels across a range of polluting industries.

Link to Image

Alex Ellinghausen

Budget papers reveal the government now expects to spend $1.9 billion less on production credits for early-stage hydrogen projects by 2030, while $300 million has also been cut from schemes to kick-start local manufacturing of solar panels and batteries.

The funding cuts mark a backdown from the government's 2022 election pitch and the creation of its hydrogen fund, which was established in early 2023 with $2 billion to help projects compete with fossil fuels.

"Our ambition is nothing less than a revival of manufacturing built off the back of the availability of clean, cheap energy," Anthony Albanese said in April 2022, before he became prime minister in May.

The government has set an ambitious climate target to cut emissions by at least 62 per cent by 2030.

Green hydrogen, a clean-burning fuel produced with renewable energy, could slash emissions by replacing polluting energy sources like diesel and gas in industrial processes.

But it still costs far more to produce than fossil fuels and hydrogen has not emerged as a viable alternative for industrial use.

Many experts argue it was overly ambitious of Labor to claim it could spark a hydrogen-led revival of the nation's manufacturing sector, while Climate Change and Energy Minister Chris Bowen maintains the government has not changed its vision.

However, green hydrogen's potential received a hammer blow in late 2024 when Donald Trump was elected US president and swiftly passed laws to end the former Biden administration's lucrative tax breaks for green technologies, while enacting sweeping measures to make it cheaper and easier for companies to extract more fossil fuels.

Link to Image

Supplied

Andrew "Twiggy" Forrest's Fortescue Metals in April 2025 announced it would not proceed with a $830 million green hydrogen plant in Arizona, blaming Trump's "shift in priorities away from green energy".

Grattan Institute energy and climate change director Alison Reeve said despite the setbacks, green hydrogen was still a viable alternative to fossil fuels, but it would not reach anywhere near the scale initially envisioned.

"The way we were thinking about hydrogen in 2019 was as a miracle fuel that would do everything. But we've come to the realisation, and this is not just in Australia but around the world, that while hydrogen can do everything, it does most things badly."

The former Coalition government was also a supporter of hydrogen and committed $500 million under the Australian Hydrogen Fund in 2019, which then Resources Minister Matt Canavan and Energy Minister Angus Taylor said would enable Australia to be a world leader in the industry.

There has been around $1.5 billion committed to green hydrogen by the federal government and several projects are under development.

Reeve said since Biden's administration raised hopes that green hydrogen could replace fossil fuels across the industrial sector, with its US$7 billion green hydrogen fund in 2021, development of the technology had sincnarrowed to the most commercially viable applications like steel and ammonia production.

"The government threw way too much money at the hydrogen industry before it had the capacity to absorb it," Reeve said. "Australia makes about a million kilos of green hydrogen a year at the moment, and it sells at up to $10 a kilogram, so it's a $10 million industry. It's not ready for billions in tax concessions."

Bowen said the government still viewed green hydrogen as essential to cut emissions, but development of the industry was now progressing amid the constraints of the global oil shock caused by the Iran war, which has strangled around 20 per cent of the world's oil supply.

"The government is addressing the urgent and emerging issues associated with the conflict in the Middle East, while continuing to advance long-term opportunities to reduce fossil fuel dependence, strengthen domestic energy security and boost sovereign capability," he said.

Most hydrogen produced across the world today is limited to "grey hydrogen", made from gas through a process that emits carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. Fortescue's ambitions focus on "green hydrogen", produced when renewable energy is used to separate water into hydrogen and oxygen, making the product emissions-free.

Cut through the noise of federal politics with news, views and expert analysis. Subscribers can sign up to our weekly Inside Politics newsletter.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and Energy Minister Chris Bowen.

Fortescue's green hydrogen electrolyser manufacturing plant in Gladstone that was abandoned in 2025.

value.Title: Trump cracks down on China -linked solar firms Trump's crackdown on China-linked solar firms stalls US factory boom
value.Overview: Richmond Times Dispatch (Virginia), A; Pg. 11A, 1029words

urn:contentItem:6JJW-8G63-S46F-Y25M-00000-00Trump cracks down on China-linked solar firms2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisAPg. 11ATrump cracks down on China-linked solar firmsTrump's crackdown on China-linked solar firms stalls US factory boomNICHOLA GROOM Reuters

Top solar companies, banks and insurers have stopped doing business with at least a half dozen U.S. panel factories because of uncertainty over whether their ties to China could disqualify them from clean-energy subsidies, according to industry executives and documents reviewed by Reuters.

The shift jeopardizes more than a third of U.S. solar capacity in factories initially built by Chinese firms. The shift also dovetails with President Donald Trump's broader efforts to block Chinese companies from the U.S. market and to slash government support for green energy.

However, the policy could backfire by imperiling growth in manufacturing jobs and power generation at a time of rising utility bills and soaring electricity demand from data centers.

Sunrun, the largest U.S. residential solar installer, is among the companies now avoiding Chinese suppliers.

"It's holding up financings of desperately needed solar and storage projects," said Keith Martin, an attorney at Norton Rose Fulbright who advises on renewable energy tax deals.

The potential effects on U.S. manufacturing underscore the difficulty of decoupling from China's global dominance of renewable energy and green technologies. This also creates a dilemma for U.S. regulators who want to block Chinese firms without imperiling U.S. solar manufacturers that depend on Chinese equipment and technology to produce competitive and affordable products.

The United States has few options for expanding renewable power beyond importing panels made by Chinese companies.

"This is undoubtedly going to continue to increase the cost of power in the United States," said Aaron Halimi, chief executive of Renewable Properties, a San Francisco developer of utility projects that has shifted most of its sourcing to Arizona-based First Solar.

The uncertainty in U.S. solar investments stems from provisions in the "One Big Beautiful Bill" that the Republican-controlled Congress passed in 2025. The legislation slashed clean-energy subsidies and restricted certain foreign countries, including China, from securing those that remained.

Trump wants to expand the U.S. power grid to fuel data centers. But experts say solar installations, combined with battery storage that clicks on when the sun isn't shining, are the quickest way to expand electricity generation because they're easier to build than gas, coal or nuclear plants.

Trump has called renewable energy unreliable and expensive and enacted policies promoting expansion of fossil fuel power sources.

A spokesperson for China's embassy in Washington criticized the U.S. restrictions as discriminatory and said Beijing would defend its companies' interests.

Chinese firms comply - with a catch

China controls about 80% of global solar equipment manufacturing, according to Wood Mackenzie. Its companies, including LONGi, Trina and others, were among the quickest to build and operate U.S. factories when former President Joe Biden's 2022 climate-change law created a tax credit for clean-energy factories.

Since then, solar equipment makers have announced nearly $43 billion in investments supporting a projected 48,000 jobs, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association.

The Trump-backed legislation restricts Chinese companies to 25% ownership stakes in plants seeking federal subsidies, imposes sourcing requirements, and prohibits "effective control" by Chinese firms. Companies say the subsidies, which include tax credits for solar manufacturing and installation, are crucial to remaining competitive.

Chinese companies have sought to comply by selling off factory stakes or otherwise restructuring. But most have preserved financial links to their U.S. plants, sometimes in the form of profit-sharing or supply deals, according to a Reuters review of corporate disclosures.

Industry officials have questions about whether those remaining links disqualify the factories from U.S. clean energy manufacturing credits. Absent guidance from the Treasury Department, installers including Sunrun are shunning these factories, while banks and insurers are withholding financing and coverage.

Paring back approved suppliers

Sunrun in January circulated a pared-down list of approved solar-panel suppliers to installation partners, according to a document seen by Reuters.

The list included only non-Chinese manufacturers such as Qcells, REC, Silfab and Elin. Previously, it had included Canadian Solar, JA Solar, Jinko, LONGiand Trina - all of which are China-linked.

"We have taken a conservative stance and do not procure equipment from manufacturers that would raise compliance concerns," Sunrun Deputy Chief Financial Officer Patrick Jobin said in a statement.

Palmetto, a North Carolina-based company that sells rooftop solar panels, is also steering clear of China-linked producers, general manager Sean Hayes said.

Meanwhile, banks including Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan and Goldman Sachs have scaled back tax-equity financing for some solar projects, according to three people familiar with the deals who spoke on condition of anonymity.

And insurers have refused to insure companies against the risk they will be barred from clean-energy tax credits, according to Antony Joyce, a tax-insurance specialist at broker Marsh.

'Clearest way to comply'

The core issue driving firms away is that Chinese companies are maintaining ties with their factories instead of making a clean break. Factories that were originally built and operated by China-linked producers account for at least 25 gigawatts of the nation's about 66 GW of operating solar module manufacturing capacity.

China's JinkoSolar, which operates a factory in Florida, and the Chinese parent company of Boviet Solar, which produces panels in North Carolina, have said they are looking for outside investors.

Illuminate USA, a joint venture between China's LONGi and Chicago-based Invenergy, reduced the Chinese firm's ownership stake in an Ohio plant built to below 25% and renegotiated its intellectual property agreement with LONGi, according to an Invenergy source.

But Invenergy is unsure if the plant, which employs around 1,700 workers, will survive. Illuminate and LONGi did not comment.

The Illuminate USA solar panel plant is seen in Pataskala, Ohio, on July 15, 2024. Megan Jelinger, Reuters The Illuminate USA solar panel plant is seen in Pataskala, Ohio, on July 15, 2024. Megan Jelinger, Reuters

urn:contentItem:6JK2-B373-RS4R-914T-00000-00Trump spurs billion-dollar cuts to Labor's green hydrogen dream2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisTrump spurs billion-dollar cuts to Labor's green hydrogen dreamMike Foley

The federal budget has revealed a major change to government's goals to revitalise local manufacturing.

Labor has slashed billions of dollars from its ambitions for hydrogen exports and a flourishing clean manufacturing sector as the Trump administration's anti-clean-energy agenda hits global investment and cripples the Albanese government's green industry goals.

This week's federal budget removed $1 billion from the program that the government said would turn Australia into a global powerhouse of green hydrogen, an energy source that burns cleanly and could replace fossil fuels across a range of polluting industries.

Link to Image

Alex Ellinghausen

Budget papers reveal the government now expects to spend $1.9 billion less on production credits for early-stage hydrogen projects by 2030, while $300 million has also been cut from schemes to kick-start local manufacturing of solar panels and batteries.

The funding cuts mark a backdown from the government's 2022 election pitch and the creation of its hydrogen fund, which was established in early 2023 with $2 billion to help projects compete with fossil fuels.

"Our ambition is nothing less than a revival of manufacturing built off the back of the availability of clean, cheap energy," Anthony Albanese said in April 2022, before he became prime minister in May.

The government has set an ambitious climate target to cut emissions by at least 62 per cent by 2030.

Green hydrogen, a clean-burning fuel produced with renewable energy, could slash emissions by replacing polluting energy sources like diesel and gas in industrial processes.

But it still costs far more to produce than fossil fuels and hydrogen has not emerged as a viable alternative for industrial use.

Many experts argue it was overly ambitious of Labor to claim it could spark a hydrogen-led revival of the nation's manufacturing sector, while Climate Change and Energy Minister Chris Bowen maintains the government has not changed its vision.

However, green hydrogen's potential received a hammer blow in late 2024 when Donald Trump was elected US president and swiftly passed laws to end the former Biden administration's lucrative tax breaks for green technologies, while enacting sweeping measures to make it cheaper and easier for companies to extract more fossil fuels.

Link to Image

Supplied

Andrew "Twiggy" Forrest's Fortescue Metals in April 2025 announced it would not proceed with a $830 million green hydrogen plant in Arizona, blaming Trump's "shift in priorities away from green energy".

Grattan Institute energy and climate change director Alison Reeve said despite the setbacks, green hydrogen was still a viable alternative to fossil fuels, but it would not reach anywhere near the scale initially envisioned.

"The way we were thinking about hydrogen in 2019 was as a miracle fuel that would do everything. But we've come to the realisation, and this is not just in Australia but around the world, that while hydrogen can do everything, it does most things badly."

The former Coalition government was also a supporter of hydrogen and committed $500 million under the Australian Hydrogen Fund in 2019, which then Resources Minister Matt Canavan and Energy Minister Angus Taylor said would enable Australia to be a world leader in the industry.

There has been around $1.5 billion committed to green hydrogen by the federal government and several projects are under development.

Reeve said since Biden's administration raised hopes that green hydrogen could replace fossil fuels across the industrial sector, with its US$7 billion green hydrogen fund in 2021, development of the technology had sincnarrowed to the most commercially viable applications like steel and ammonia production.

"The government threw way too much money at the hydrogen industry before it had the capacity to absorb it," Reeve said. "Australia makes about a million kilos of green hydrogen a year at the moment, and it sells at up to $10 a kilogram, so it's a $10 million industry. It's not ready for billions in tax concessions."

Bowen said the government still viewed green hydrogen as essential to cut emissions, but development of the industry was now progressing amid the constraints of the global oil shock caused by the Iran war, which has strangled around 20 per cent of the world's oil supply.

"The government is addressing the urgent and emerging issues associated with the conflict in the Middle East, while continuing to advance long-term opportunities to reduce fossil fuel dependence, strengthen domestic energy security and boost sovereign capability," he said.

Most hydrogen produced across the world today is limited to "grey hydrogen", made from gas through a process that emits carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. Fortescue's ambitions focus on "green hydrogen", produced when renewable energy is used to separate water into hydrogen and oxygen, making the product emissions-free.

Cut through the noise of federal politics with news, views and expert analysis. Subscribers can sign up to our weekly Inside Politics newsletter.

Tue May 12, 2026: Pinnacle West Capital's Energy Transition: A Strategic Overview

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation, Arizona's largest electricity provider, faces evolving grid demands as the state accelerates renewable energy adoption and manages rate pressures in a competitive market. As of 12th May 2026, Pinnacle West Capital operates through its subsidiary Arizona Public Service, which serves approximately 1.3 million customers across central and southern Arizona.

The company functions as a holding entity for Arizona Public Service Company, one of the largest vertically integrated electric utilities in the United States. Pinnacle West Capital generates, transmits, and distributes electricity over a service territory that spans roughly 14,400 square miles. Revenue primarily comes from regulated rates, which are approved by the Arizona Corporation Commission, ensuring a stable revenue stream while necessitating ongoing rate case filings to accommodate inflation and infrastructure needs.

Pinnacle West Capital's revenue model is heavily reliant on electricity sales to residential and commercial customers, with industrial and agricultural segments contributing additional diversity. The company's generation portfolio includes the Palo Verde Nuclear Generating Station, one of the largest nuclear facilities in the U.S., which remains a key asset for baseload power. Meanwhile, increasing investments in renewable energy, including solar and wind projects, are gaining traction in response to changing policy and customer preferences in Arizona.

The utility's capital expenditure strategy focuses on infrastructure modernization and grid reliability, with a strong emphasis on transmission and distribution system upgrades. Pinnacle West Capital has been proactive in pursuing rate recovery mechanisms to support investments in smart grid technology and renewable energy integration. The demographic growth in Arizona's metropolitan areas underpins a long-term demand for electricity, allowing the company to achieve revenue growth independent of rate increases.

For U.S. investors, Pinnacle West Capital provides exposure to the regulated utility sector, typically characterized by lower volatility and attractive dividend yields. The company's strategic positioning within a rapidly growing Arizona market, combined with its commitment to renewable energy, aligns with evolving investor interests in environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors. As Pinnacle West Capital continues to adapt to changes in energy demand, its focus on sustainable growth and reliable service will remain crucial for delivering value to shareholders.

SECTION 2 TODAY'S BULLISH SIGNALS

2.1 Uptrend

Today's Volatility:

The 5% discount to 12-month high of $104.20 against the 20% premium to 12-month low of $82.89 suggests the stock continues to push for new highs.

Today its volatility (highest price minus lowest price/lowest price) of 1.6% equalled the average daily volatility, up from 1.3% on Tuesday and 1.0% on Monday. Price/Moving Average Price of 1.08:

- The Price/MAP 200 for Pinnacle West Capital Corporation is 1.08. Being higher than 1 is a bullish indicator. The stock is trading above both its MAPs and the 50-day MAP of $100.71 is higher than the 200-day MAP of $91.87, a second bullish indicator. The 200-day MAP has increased to $91.87, a third bullish indicator.

2.2 Other Bullish Signals

MCap/Total Assets:

- Tobin's Q Ratio, defined as MCap divided by Total Assets, is 0.4. Compared with the rest of the market the stock is undervalued and ranks in the top quartile of stocks by value of Q Ratio.

Source: Pinnacle West Capital Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 25, 2026, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/7286/000076462226000011/pnw-20251231.htm and Pinnacle West Capital Corporation's website: https://www.pinnaclewest.com/investors/default.aspx. company (Pinnacle West Capital Corporation), Ticker(PNW)

2.3 Oversold/Bullish Signals:

- The stock is oversold according to the Williams % R indicator of -81.3, suggesting the price is close to its 14-day low of $97.90.

- The Stochastic indicator of 12.4 has pierced the oversold line of 20; this indicates the price is close to its 14-day low and is likely to revert to an uptrend.

Source: New York Stock Exchange: https://www.nasdaq.com/market-activity/stocks/PNW, company (Pinnacle West Capital Corporation), Ticker(PNW)

SECTION 3 ONGOING BULLISH PARAMETERS

3.1 Created Market Value [CMV] past 18 yrs of $5.8 billion

- Market Capitalization has increased by $8.2 billion from $3.7 billion to $11.9 billion in the last 18 years. This increase comprises cumulative retained earnings (RETE) of $2.4 billion and Created Market Value of $5.8 billion. The Created Market Value multiple, defined by the change in MCap for every $1 of retained earnings is exemplary at $3.37.

3.2 Satisfies three criteria of Benjamin Graham

-""Total debt less than tangible book value""; total debt of USD757.0 million is less than tangible book value of USD2.9 billion.

-""Total debt equal or less than twice the net quick liquidation value""; total debt of USD757.0 million is 0.8 times the net liquidation value of USD946.70 million.

-""Stability of growth in earnings over the last 5 years, defined as no more than two declines of 5% or greater in year-end earnings""; there has been only 1 decline in earnings in the last 5 years.

Source: Pinnacle West Capital Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 25, 2026, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/7286/000076462226000011/pnw-20251231.htm and Pinnacle West Capital Corporation's website: https://www.pinnaclewest.com/investors/default.aspx. company (Pinnacle West Capital Corporation), Ticker(PNW)

SECTION 4 TODAY'S BEARISH SIGNALS

4.1 Other Bearish Signals

- Total Liabilities/EBITDA of 8.3 is more than 5, this compares unfavourably with the Joseph Piotroski benchmark of 5.

- Net profit margin has averaged 11.7% in the last 3 years. This is lower than the sector average of 14.2% and suggests a low margin of safety.

- As per the Du Pont analysis, Return on Equity of 8.9% is lower than sector average of 9.9%. This is computed as net profit margin of 11.8% times asset turnover [sales/assets] of 0.18 times leverage factor [total assets/shareholders' equity] of 4.2.

Source: Pinnacle West Capital Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 25, 2026, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/7286/000076462226000011/pnw-20251231.htm and Pinnacle West Capital Corporation's website: https://www.pinnaclewest.com/investors/default.aspx. company (Pinnacle West Capital Corporation), Ticker(PNW)

Source: New York Stock Exchange: https://www.nasdaq.com/market-activity/stocks/PNW, company (Pinnacle West Capital Corporation), Ticker(PNW)

SECTION 5 ONGOING BEARISH PARAMETERS

5.1 Satisfies 3 out of 9 criteria of Joseph Piotroski [pass mark 5]:

- Positive net income.

- Positive operating cashflow.

- Good quality of earnings [operating cashflow exceeds net income].

But does not meet the following 6 criteria of Joseph Piotroski:

- Return on Assets improvement.

- Improvement in long-term debt to total assets.

- Improvement in current ratio.

- Total shares on issue unchanged (or reduction in total shares on issue).

- Improvement in gross margin.

- Improvement in asset turnover.

Source: Pinnacle West Capital Corporation's, 10-K filing dated February 25, 2026, available at the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission website: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/7286/000076462226000011/pnw-20251231.htm and Pinnacle West Capital Corporation's website: https://www.pinnaclewest.com/investors/default.aspx. company (Pinnacle West Capital Corporation), Ticker(PNW)

Source: New York Stock Exchange: https://www.nasdaq.com/market-activity/stocks/PNW, company (Pinnacle West Capital Corporation), Ticker(PNW)

SECTION 6 CORPORATE PROFILE

6.1 Activities

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation, a Phoenix, Arizona-based holding company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, primarily operates through its subsidiary Arizona Public Service Company (APS), which generates, transmits, and distributes electricity to millions of customers in Arizona. The company's core business focuses on providing reliable energy solutions, including a mix of coal, nuclear, natural gas, and renewable sources, serving residential, commercial, and industrial sectors. In recent years, the fastest-growing segments have been renewable energy initiatives, particularly solar power, driven by increasing demand for sustainable options, regulatory incentives, and technological advancements. This strategic emphasis on clean energy has bolstered Pinnacle West's portfolio, enhancing operational efficiency and long-term growth potential in the competitive utility market, while maintaining a commitment to environmental stewardship and grid reliability. It is the NYSE's 14th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

It has a moderate relative ROE of 8.9% and ROA of 2.1%. The company paid a dividend of $3.62 in the last twelve months. The dividend yield is moderate at 3.7%.

urn:contentItem:6JH5-ND63-SC00-T4FR-00000-00Salt River Project Partners with NextEra Energy Resources for Major Solar Initiative2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisSalt River Project Partners with NextEra Energy Resources for Major Solar Initiative

AMERICAN DAILY STOCK REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

05 May 2026 07:45 EDT

Salt River Project has announced a transformative power purchase agreement with NextEra Energy Resources, marking a significant step in the utility's renewable energy strategy. The deal, revealed on 4th May 2026, encompasses the development of 3,000 MW of solar generation alongside 1,000 MW of battery storage capacity, with construction slated to take place across Arizona and completion expected by 2027.

This initiative aligns with Salt River Project's broader objective to phase out coal-fired power generation by 2032. The solar and storage capacities combined will provide electricity for approximately 675,000 homes at peak output. Notably, the projects will utilize bifacial solar modules and tracking systems, which are designed to maximize energy capture in the challenging desert Southwest climate.

The 1,000 MW of battery storage is strategically aimed at addressing the evening peak period when solar production wanes while household energy demand rises. By storing solar energy for later use, Salt River Project intends to minimize its reliance on natural gas plants, thereby enhancing grid stability during periods of extreme heat. Furthermore, the utility anticipates that this significant buildout will not only generate tax revenue for local communities but also create numerous construction jobs over the next three years.

As of early 2026, Arizona has exceeded 11.4 GW of installed solar capacity, according to data from the Solar Energy Industries Association, with over 9,700 individuals employed in the state's solar sector. Solar power currently represents about 16% of Arizona's electricity mix, with expectations to add an additional 13.8 GW of capacity within the next five years. This expansion is indicative of the state's commitment to renewable energy and its potential for further growth in the sector.

In conjunction with this agreement, a comprehensive report on the solar cells and light-emitting diodes industry in the United States provides valuable insights into market dynamics, tracking demand, supply, and trade flows. The report benchmarks prices, margins, and trade routes, offering critical data for stakeholders in the solar landscape. This information is designed to aid manufacturers, distributors, and investors in navigating the evolving solar market and making informed decisions as they plan for the future.

SECTION 1 NEXTERA ENERGY RECENT PRESS RELEASES

February 18: NextEra Energy quarterly dividends up 10% sequentially from previous quarter

NextEra Energy today announced a quarterly dividend of 62.32c per share for Q4/2025; up 10% sequentially from the previous quarter (Q3/2025) dividend of 56.65c. The ex-dividend date is Friday, February 27, 2026 and the record date is Friday, February 27, 2026 and it is payable on Monday, March 16.

November 18, 2025: NextEra Energy substantial shareholder sells

NextEra Energy (NYSE:NEE) substantial shareholder Pimentel Armando Jr sold 145,140 shares worth $12,178,697 on November 17. The selling price was $83.91.

October 27, 2025: NextEra Energy quarterly dividends steady

NextEra Energy today announced a quarterly dividend of 56.65c per share for Q3/2025; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q2/2025) dividend of 56.65c. The ex-dividend date is Friday, November 21, 2025 and the record date is Friday, November 21, 2025 and it is payable on Monday, December 15.

SECTION 2 NEXTERA ENERGY ACTIVITIES

Activities

Nextera Energy, Inc., a Fortune 200 company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, is a leading clean energy provider in the United States, specializing in the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity. As one of the world's largest producers of renewable energy, its core operations encompass a diverse portfolio that includes wind, solar, nuclear, and natural gas power plants, serving millions of customers across Florida and other regions. The company's fastest-growing segments are its renewable energy initiatives, particularly utility-scale solar and wind projects, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing demand for sustainable power and supportive regulatory environments. This growth is driven by strategic investments in innovative technologies and infrastructure, positioning Nextera as a pioneer in the energy transition. With a strong commitment to operational efficiency and environmental stewardship, Nextera continues to deliver reliable energy solutions while advancing its goal of reducing carbon emissions. It is the NYSE's largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

urn:contentItem:6JGJ-JTH3-RSH0-R112-00000-00Mitsui & Co. Ltd. (OT:MITSY (ADR)) Secures $850 Million Cobalt Offtake With EVelution Energy In Arizona2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisMitsui & Co. Ltd. (OT:MITSY (ADR)) Secures $850 Million Cobalt Offtake With EVelution Energy In Arizona

DAILY ADR AND DEPOSITORY RECEIPT REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

01 May 2026 12:31 EDT

Mitsui & Co. Ltd. (OT:MITSY (ADR)) Posts An $850 Million Cobalt Offtake To Purchase The Majority Of Cobalt Metal From A Planned Solar Powered Processing Plant In Yuma County, A Deal Disclosed On 29th April 2026

The Binding Agreement Covers Nearly All Cobalt Metal Production From The Facility While Negotiations For Cobalt Sulfate Remain Ongoing With EVelution Energy, The Developer Behind The Plant, Which Plans To Begin Supplying Mitsui With Up To 3,000 Metric Tons Of Contained Cobalt Per Year At First, With A Target Of About 7,000 Metric Tons Annually

Construction Is Slated To Start In Early 2027 And End By The End Of 2029, And The Project Is Expected To Generate More Than $750 Million In Local Economic Impact And Create About 3,300 Jobs Across Direct, Indirect, And Induced Channels, As Regulators Scrutinize Water Use In An Arid Region

SECTION 1 MITSUI & CO. ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 Mitsui & Co. Activities

Activities

Mitsui & Co. Ltd., a prominent Japanese conglomerate and one of the world's largest general trading companies, focuses on global trading, investment, and development across a diverse portfolio. Its core activities encompass the import, export, and distribution of commodities, including energy resources, metals, machinery, chemicals, and consumer goods, alongside strategic investments in infrastructure, logistics, and technology. The company leverages its extensive network in over 90 countries to facilitate international business and sustainable growth. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy, such as solar and wind projects, digital transformation services, and healthcare innovations, fueled by rising global demand for clean energy and advanced technologies. This diversification positions Mitsui as a key player in addressing environmental challenges and economic development worldwide. It is the International Depository Receipt (IDR) market's 2nd largest Distribution/Wholesale company by market capitalisation.

SECTION 2 CORPORATE PROFILE

2.1 Activities

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation, a Phoenix, Arizona-based holding company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, primarily operates through its subsidiary Arizona Public Service Company (APS), which generates, transmits, and distributes electricity to millions of customers in Arizona. The company's core business focuses on providing reliable energy solutions, including a mix of coal, nuclear, natural gas, and renewable sources, serving residential, commercial, and industrial sectors. In recent years, the fastest-growing segments have been renewable energy initiatives, particularly solar power, driven by increasing demand for sustainable options, regulatory incentives, and technological advancements. This strategic emphasis on clean energy has bolstered Pinnacle West's portfolio, enhancing operational efficiency and long-term growth potential in the competitive utility market, while maintaining a commitment to environmental stewardship and grid reliability. It is the NYSE's 14th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

November 03 2025: Pinnacle West Reports 2025 Third-Quarter Financial Results

Customer growth, summer heat drive increase in sales and usage

Operating performance, customer reliability remain strong

Company expands support for community and utility bill assistance programs

PHOENIX-- Pinnacle West Capital Corp. (NYSE: PNW) today reported consolidated net income attributable to common shareholders for the 2025 third quarter of $413.2 million, or $3.39 per diluted share of common stock. This result compares with consolidated net income of $395.0 million, or $3.37 per share of common stock, for the same period a year ago. However, through the first three quarters of the current year, the company's earnings were 2.4% lower than in the first nine months of 2024.

The 2025 third-quarter results reflect an increase of about $18 million compared to the 2024 third quarter, primarily as a result of increased customer usage, customer growth and related pricing; higher transmission service revenues; lower operations and maintenance expenses; higher AFUDC; lower depreciation and amortization expenses mostly due to operations ceasing at Cholla, partially offset by increased plant additions and intangible assets. These positive factors were partially offset by the unfavorable impacts of weather effects versus the same period a year ago; higher interest charges; lower pension and other post-retirement non-service credits; higher income taxes due to higher pre-tax income and lower tax credits.

"Driven by one of the fastest-growing service territories in the country and the third-hottest Arizona summer on record, we experienced an increase in retail sales that helped contribute to solid third-quarter financial results," said Pinnacle West Chairman, President and Chief Executive Officer Ted Geisler. "In addition, our employees continued to do what they do best: maintaining reliable electric service for our 1.4 million customers.

October 02 2025: Events Calendar: Pinnacle West Sets Date for 2025 Third-Quarter Financial Results, Webcast/Conference Call

PHOENIX- Pinnacle West Capital Corp. (NYSE: PNW) announced today that it plans to release its 2025 third-quarter financial results before the U.S. financial markets open on Monday, Nov. 3, 2025.

That same day at 11 a.m. ET (9 a.m. Arizona time), management will host a live webcast and conference call to discuss financial results and recent developments.

To access the live session:

Join the webcast at www.pinnaclewest.com/presentations for audio of the call and slides; or

Dial (888) 506-0062 or (973) 528-0011 for international callers and enter participant access code 502381. To access the replay:

Visit www.pinnaclewest.com/presentations within 30 days for the webcast recording; or

An audio recording will be available by phone until 11:59 p.m. ET, Monday, Nov. 10, 2025, by calling (877) 481-4010 in the U.S. and Canada or (919) 882-2331 internationally and entering replay passcode 53038.

Pinnacle West Capital Corp., an energy holding company based in Phoenix, has consolidated assets of more than $29 billion, about 6,500 megawatts of generating capacity and approximately 6,400 employees in Arizona and New Mexico. Through its principal subsidiary, Arizona Public Service, the company provides retail electricity service to about 1.4 million Arizona homes and businesses. For more information about Pinnacle West, visit the company's website at pinnaclewest.com.

SECTION 4 RECENT NEWS

May 11 2026: Pinnacle West Capital Reveals Growth Plans Amid 2025 Rate Case

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation has unveiled an ambitious growth strategy, targeting a long-term earnings-per-share (EPS) growth of 5% to 7%. This announcement comes as the company also details a $10.35 billion capital plan for its Arizona Public Service (APS) subsidiary, covering the period from 2025 to 2028. As of 11th May 2026, the focus on these initiatives demonstrates Pinnacle West Capital's commitment to expanding its footprint in the rapidly growing Arizona market.

The company is actively pursuing a significant rate case for 2025, requesting a $694 million increase in total revenue requirements. This request is underpinned by an adjusted Arizona Corporation Commission (ACC) rate base of $13.1 billion as of December 31, 2024, alongside a proposed allowed return on equity of 10.70%. Pinnacle West Capital aims to enhance its revenue streams further while navigating the regulatory landscape that governs its operations.

Pinnacle West Capital's business model centers on regulated rate-base growth, capitalizing on state-approved investments in transmission, distribution, and generation. This strategy not only secures higher allowed returns but also provides long-term revenue visibility. The company's APS subsidiary serves approximately 1.5 million customers in Arizona, making it a key player in the region's energy market. With an annual dividend growth record of about 3.7%, Pinnacle West Capital remains attractive to income-oriented investors seeking stable cash flows.

Looking ahead, the company anticipates weather-normalized retail sales growth of 4% to 6% for 2026 and has raised its long-term sales growth forecast to 5% to 7% through 2030. This optimistic outlook is supported by robust demand in Arizona and ongoing customer growth. Pinnacle West Capital plans significant investments in new gas generation, upgrades to the Palo Verde nuclear facility, and potential transmission capital expenditures exceeding $6 billion by 2035, all designed to support its EPS growth objectives.

In summary, Pinnacle West Capital is positioning itself as a growth-oriented regulated utility with a clear trajectory for rate-base expansion and a compelling roadmap for the future. While the company faces typical regulatory, interest-rate, and capital-intensity risks associated with the utility sector, its strategic initiatives and focus on Arizona's expanding electricity demand may appeal to U.S. investors. The balance of steady cash flows against potential regulatory uncertainties will be crucial as Pinnacle West Capital aims to finance approximately $8 billion of funding from 2026 to 2028, including $1.0 to $1.2 billion of equity.

Recent News 2 of 5, Source: HourlyBusinessNews

May 10 2026: Pinnacle West Capital Reports Strong Q1 Earnings Amid Arizona's Record Heat

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation reported first-quarter earnings that significantly exceeded market expectations on May 4th, 2026, as record-breaking warmth in Arizona drove higher energy demand. The utility holding company posted earnings per share of 27 cents, surpassing the consensus estimate by analysts by 1 cent.

Total revenue for Pinnacle West reached $1.15 billion for the quarter, outperforming the anticipated $1.08 billion figure. This growth can be attributed to increased transmission revenues and a notable rise in customer usage trends, reflecting the impact of the unusually warm weather.

Operational efficiency also played a role in the positive financial results, with lower maintenance expenses contributing to the bottom line. However, Pinnacle West noted that these gains were partially offset by rising interest expenses, depreciation costs, and income taxes, which are common challenges for utility companies.

CEO Ted Geisler commented on the performance, attributing it to the unusual climate patterns affecting the region's energy consumption. He stated, "Arizona experienced its warmest winter on record, with elevated temperatures continuing into spring, resulting in stronger-than-normal energy usage from retail customers during the quarter."

Following the financial disclosure, Barclays analyst Nicholas Campanella adjusted the firm's outlook on Pinnacle West, raising the price target for the New York Stock Exchange-listed firm to $102 from $101. Despite this strong start, the company maintained its full-year earnings guidance between $4.55 and $4.75 per share, while other market observers remain cautious, noting prior adjustments to price targets.

May 09 2026: Pinnacle West Capital Reports Q1 Profit Surge Amid Arizona Growth

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation has reported a significant return to quarterly profitability for the first three months of 2026, fueled by rising transmission revenues and customer growth in Arizona. On 9th May 2026, the company announced a consolidated net income attributable to common shareholders of $32.9 million, equating to $0.27 per diluted share, a marked improvement from a net loss of $4.6 million, or $0.04 per share, during the same period in 2025.

This turnaround was largely driven by factors including increased transmission revenues, a reduction in operations and maintenance expenses, favorable weather conditions, and ongoing growth in customer numbers and electricity usage across Arizona. However, these gains were partially offset by higher interest charges, income taxes, and depreciation and amortization costs.

As of early May 2026, Pinnacle West Capital's shares are trading close to $104 on the New York Stock Exchange, nearing the upper limit of its 52-week trading range, which spans from approximately $85 to $105. The stock has seen a year-to-date gain of about 17%, indicating a positive investor sentiment towards the company’s recovery and the favorable economic landscape in Arizona.

Pinnacle West Capital operates primarily through its subsidiary, Arizona Public Service, the largest electric utility in the state, providing retail electricity to approximately 1.5 million homes and businesses. Its business model focuses on regulated rate-base growth, where state-sanctioned investments in energy infrastructure yield stable returns, making it an appealing option for income-oriented investors seeking consistent cash flows.

Looking ahead, analysts anticipate that continued population and economic growth in Arizona, driven by significant investments in sectors such as semiconductors and data centers, will sustain elevated electricity demand. While Pinnacle West Capital's recent performance underscores its importance as a regulated utility in the Southwest, potential headwinds include rising interest costs and regulatory risks that investors should consider when evaluating its future prospects.

May 09 2026: Barclays Adjusts Price Target for Pinnacle West to $102

In a notable development for investors, Barclays has raised its price target on Pinnacle West Capital Corporation (NYSE:PNW) to $102, reflecting a slight increase from the previous target of $101. This adjustment came after the company reported its first-quarter earnings, with Barclays analyst Nicholas Campanella affirming an Equal Weight rating for Pinnacle West. The report was released on the 9th of May, 2026, following the company's quarterly performance that showcased promising trends.

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation delivered a first-quarter earnings per share (EPS) of 27 cents, surpassing the consensus estimate by 1 cent, while its revenue reached $1.15 billion versus the expected $1.08 billion. The company's performance was bolstered by several factors, including increased transmission revenues and lower operational expenses. CEO Ted Geisler noted that Arizona experienced its warmest winter on record, leading to higher energy consumption from retail customers during this period.

Despite the positive results, Pinnacle West acknowledged certain challenges, including increased interest expenses, income taxes, and depreciation costs. Nevertheless, the company has maintained its full-year EPS outlook for 2026, projecting a range of $4.55 to $4.75, which aligns closely with consensus estimates of $4.70. This cautious optimism indicates underlying confidence in the company’s ability to navigate market conditions effectively.

In contrast to Barclays' optimistic outlook, Morgan Stanley's analyst David Arcaro recently adjusted Pinnacle West's price target downward, from $99 to $98, while also maintaining an Equal Weight rating. This reflects a broader reassessment of North American regulated utilities and independent power producers, suggesting a mixed sentiment among analysts regarding the future performance of Pinnacle West and its peers in the energy sector.

As Pinnacle West Capital Corporation continues to provide essential retail and wholesale electric services in Arizona, the market is keenly watching its strategic moves and financial performance. Investors are advised to weigh these insights carefully as they consider their positions in PNW, particularly in the context of evolving market dynamics and competition in the energy landscape.

May 08 2026: Pinnacle West Capital: Q1 2026 Earnings Signal Recovery Near 52-Week High

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation (PNW) has announced a robust return to profitability in the first quarter of 2026, as favorable weather conditions and increased transmission revenues have driven the company's financial performance. The company reported consolidated net income of $32.9 million, or $0.27 per diluted share, for the three months ending on 31st March 2026. This marks a significant recovery from a net loss of $4.6 million, or $0.04 per diluted share, in the same period a year earlier, showcasing an impressive turnaround of approximately $38 million.

The positive financial results can be attributed to several factors, including higher transmission revenues, reduced operations and maintenance expenses, and a notable uptick in customer usage spurred by hotter-than-normal weather. Additionally, customer growth in Arizona, where Pinnacle West primarily operates, has bolstered sales and usage rates. These developments have helped PNW shares trade close to their 52-week high, reflecting increased investor confidence in the company's recovery trajectory.

Pinnacle West Capital operates primarily through its subsidiary, Arizona Public Service (APS), which provides retail and wholesale electric services across Arizona and parts of New Mexico. The company's business model is heavily regulated, which ensures predictable cash flows while exposing it to regulatory risks. With consolidated assets totaling approximately $31 billion and a generating capacity of around 6,200 megawatts, Pinnacle West is well-positioned to meet the growing energy demands of its customer base, which has increased by 2.2% in the first quarter of 2026.

Looking ahead, analysts note that ongoing investments in infrastructure, including new transmission and distribution projects, are expected to further enhance the company’s regulated rate base. This could support higher allowed returns and future revenue growth, despite potential challenges from rising interest rates and regulatory decisions. The company’s current market capitalization stands at around $12.5 billion, positioning Pinnacle West Capital as a mid-cap utility in the U.S. market. Its stock offers a quarterly yield of approximately 3.5%, appealing to income-focused investors.

In conclusion, Pinnacle West Capital's strong earnings rebound in the first quarter of 2026 has not only lifted its stock price but also reinforced its position as a stable, regulated utility in a rapidly growing region. However, potential investors should remain mindful of the inherent risks associated with regulatory changes and financing costs, which could impact the company's earnings and valuation moving forward. With a competitive price-to-earnings ratio, Pinnacle West presents an intriguing option for those seeking steady growth and income in the utility sector.

value.Title: Hobbs vetoes 'unbalanced and reckless' Republican budget, calls for negotiations to resume

urn:contentItem:6JHB-1CT3-S5CJ-343G-00000-00Hobbs vetoes 'unbalanced and reckless' Republican budget, calls for negotiations to resume2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisHobbs vetoes 'unbalanced and reckless' Republican budget, calls for negotiations to resume

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Caitlin Sievers

May 5th, 2026 ( Arizona Mirror - Delivered by Newstex )

(Photo via iStock / Getty Images Plus)

Arizona's Democratic governor on Tuesday vetoed a Republican budget plan after it passed both chambers of the state legislature on party line votes, saying it would bring 'chaos and dysfunction' to the Grand Canyon State.

Republicans, who have a majority in both chambers, introduced their $17.9 billion budget plan on April 23 and quickly put it up for a vote. It passed the Arizona House of Representatives April 29 and the Arizona Senate on May 4.

The Arizona House of Representatives sent the budget to Gov. Katie Hobbs on Tuesday, and she swiftly vetoed it, as expected.

'This budget is unbalanced and reckless. With it, Arizona would default on our debt obligations, endanger vulnerable children, slash critical public safety funding, and pay for tax breaks to billionaires, data centers and special interests by kicking Arizonans off their healthcare and taking food off their tables,' Hobbs said in a written statement. 'Arizonans cannot afford chaotic and dysfunctional Washington-style budgeting in our state government.'

GET THE MORNING HEADLINES.

SUBSCRIBE

The GOP budget 'has a long list of problems,' Hobbs said, including more than $600 million in tax cuts for the wealthy and data centers while at the same time kicking an estimated 200,000 Arizonans off of Medicaid and food aid.

'While forcing devastating cuts on Arizonans, the legislature has refused to sweep their own $28 million slush fund, including $6 million that may be used to replace their carpets, upgrade their media studio, and renovate their offices,' the governor said.

She called on Republican lawmakers to negotiate a spending plan with her.

'Let's get back to the negotiating table and get serious about delivering for Arizonans. I am ready when you are,' Hobbs said.

When exactly that might happen is unclear: Lawmakers on Tuesday adjourned for the remainder of May and won't return to work until June 1.

Republicans touted the $500 million in tax cuts in their budget plan, which conforms with many of the changes made at the federal level by President Donald Trump's 'One Big Beautiful Bill,' as well as the continuation of existing funding for K-12 education and public safety.

It would sweep money from numerous funds and make 5% cuts to most state agencies to pay for the tax cuts, as well as increase the portion that state employees contribute to their health insurance by 20% over the next three years.

Democrats criticized the Republican budget for its cuts to state government, as well as its increased restrictions on the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, formerly known as food stamps, and for the Arizona Health Care Cost Containment System, the state's Medicaid program.

But Democrats mostly focused on the Republican tax cuts that they said would benefit the wealthy, describing the Republican budget as 'corporations first, Arizonans last.'

The Republican budget maintains tax incentives for the data centers needed for generative artificial intelligence, but cuts tax breaks for renewable energy providers.

Hobbs's budget proposal includes about $800 million more in spending than the Republican plan, and would cut data center tax breaks and tax lucrative sports betting operations at a higher rate.

Republicans had plenty of their own criticism for Hobbs's budget plan, when she unveiled it in January. They panned her proposal for relying on money that the state might not ever receive.

That includes $300 million in Proposition 123 funding for K-12 education that would need to be approved by voters in November and more than $760 million from the federal government to reimburse the state for its work securing the border that could go to other states.

Voters approved Prop. 123 in 2016, to increase the amount of money set aside from the state's land trust for public schools. After Prop. 123 expired, the legislature voted to use the general fund to backfill the lost $300 million.

Hobbs walked away from budget negotiations more than a month ago after Republicans said that sending an extension of Prop. 123 to voters this year was off the table. If voters approved it, the continuation of the proposition would alleviate the state's budget woes for this year, according to the governor's budget staff.

House Minority Leader Oscar De Los Santos urged his GOP colleagues to begin working with Hobbs immediately to hammer out a budget deal now that their partisan spending package has been vetoed.

'We must stay here and pass a budget to make life affordable for Arizona who are struggling to stay afloat with Donald Trump's economic chaos and failing presidency,' he said in a written statement.

Following the veto, legislative Republicans and Democrats, alongside Hobbs, will have to work together to create a negotiated budget that can pass both chambers with bipartisan support and get a signature from Hobbs by the June 30 deadline.

Democrats have said they can get behind some pieces of the Republican budget, including middle class tax cuts, funding for free school lunches, no taxes on tips and child care tax credits.

YOU MAKE OUR WORK POSSIBLE.

SUPPORT

value.Title: South32 Ltd (OTC:SOUHY (ADR)) Posts ADEQ 401 Water Quality Certification Waiver for Hermosa Project

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-J7R3-RS50-S41F-00000-00South32 Ltd (OTC:SOUHY (ADR)) Posts ADEQ 401 Water Quality Certification Waiver for Hermosa Project2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisSouth32 Ltd (OTC:SOUHY (ADR)) Posts ADEQ 401 Water Quality Certification Waiver for Hermosa Project

DAILY ADR AND DEPOSITORY RECEIPT REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

13 May 2026 15:01 EDT

On May 6, 2026, the Arizona Department of Environmental Quality granted a Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification waiver to South32 Hermosa, after reviewing 22 public comments submitted between Feb 18 and Mar 20. The waiver will allow the Hermosa mine near Patagonia, Arizona, to proceed while adhering to water quality protections.

ADEQ indicated it carefully reviewed input during the public comment period and issued the waiver accordingly. The approval reflects the state's effort to balance mineral resource development with environmental safeguards in water-scarce Arizona. South32 emphasizes its plan to develop critical mineral resources within state and federal guidelines and to maintain ongoing community engagement.

The Hermosa project is expected to bolster local economic development through job creation and infrastructure improvements. South32 will continue transparency with regulators and stakeholders as it advances its plans, reflecting a broader industry trend toward sustainable mining practices.

South32 Stock Dashboard Adr:SOUHY

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1 SOUTH32 ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 South32 Activities

Activities

South32 Limited is a global mining and metals company headquartered in Perth, Australia, with operations across Australia, Southern Africa, and South America. The company focuses on the production and sale of a diverse portfolio of commodities, including bauxite, alumina, aluminium, manganese, metallurgical coal, silver, lead, zinc, and nickel. Its core business activities emphasize sustainable resource extraction and processing, supporting industries such as construction, energy, and technology. Among its fastest-growing segments, manganese production has surged due to rising demand in steel manufacturing and battery technologies, while aluminium operations have expanded amid the global shift toward renewable energy and electric vehicles. South32 prioritizes operational efficiency and environmental stewardship, positioning itself as a key player in the evolving metals market through strategic investments and divestitures from less sustainable assets. It is the International Depository Receipt (IDR) market's 14th largest Mining company by market capitalisation.

value.Title: United States : Trump's crackdown on China -linked solar firms stalls U.S. factory boom
value.Overview: Asia News Monitor, 1388words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJK-N943-S7BH-522V-00000-00United States: Trump's crackdown on China-linked solar firms stalls U.S. factory boom2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisUnited States: Trump's crackdown on China-linked solar firms stalls U.S. factory boomBangkokFULL TEXT

Top solar companies, banks and insurers have stopped doing business with at least a half dozen recently built U.S. panel factories because of uncertainty over whether their ties to China could disqualify them from clean-energy subsidies, according to industry executives and documents reviewed by Reuters.

The shift, driven by new Trump administration policies, jeopardizes more than a third of U.S. solar capacity in factories initially built by Chinese firms. Details of how the policy uncertainty is driving installers and insurers away from U.S. solar factories with China ties have not been previously reported.

The emerging effects dovetail with U.S. President Donald Trump's broader efforts to block Chinese companies from the U.S. market and to slash government support for green energy. However, the policy could backfire by imperiling growth in U.S. manufacturing jobs and power generation at a time of rising utility bills and soaring electricity demand from data centers serving the artificial intelligence industry, industry experts say.

Sunrun, the largest U.S. residential solar installer, is among the companies now avoiding Chinese suppliers.

It's holding up financings of desperately needed solar and storage projects, said Keith Martin, an attorney at Norton Rose Fulbright who advises on renewable energy tax deals.

The potentially far-reaching effects on U.S. manufacturing underscore the difficulty of decoupling from China's global dominance of renewable energy and green technologies, driven largely by Beijing's own heavy subsidies for Chinese firms.

The global reach of China's industrial policy creates a dilemma for U.S. regulators who want to block Chinese firms without imperiling U.S. solar manufacturers that depend on Chinese equipment and technology to produce competitive and affordable products.

Without robust growth in domestic solar manufacturing, the United States has few options for expanding renewable power beyond importing panels made by Chinese companies, which will lead to higher prices, U.S. executives say.

This is undoubtedly going to continue to increase the cost of power in the United States, said Aaron Halimi, chief executive of Renewable Properties, a San Francisco developer of small-scale utility projects that has shifted most of its sourcing to Tempe, Arizona-based First Solar to avoid suppliers with China links.

The fresh uncertainty in U.S. solar investments stems from provisions in the Trump-backed One Big Beautiful Bill that the Republican-controlled Congress passed in 2025.

The legislation slashed Biden-era clean-energy subsidies and restricted certain foreign countries, including China, from securing those that remained. The U.S. Treasury Department has yet to provide full guidance on how the law will be implemented, and a department spokesperson declined to give a timeline for when that guidance would be published.

Trump wants to rapidly expand the U.S. power grid to fuel American data centers. But power-industry experts say solar installations, combined with battery storage that clicks on when the sun isn't shining, are the quickest way to expand electricity generation because they're easier to build than gas, coal or nuclear plants.

Trump has called renewable energy unreliable and expensive and enacted policies promoting expansion of fossil fuel power sources.

The White House did not respond to a request for comment.

A spokesperson for China's embassy in Washington criticized the U.S. restrictions as discriminatory and said Beijing would defend its companies' interests.

CHINESE SOLAR FIRMS COMPLY - WITH A CATCH

China controls about 80% of global solar equipment manufacturing, according to Wood Mackenzie. Its companies, including LONGi, Trina, and others, were among the quickest to build and operate U.S. factories when former President Joe Biden's 2022 climate-change law created a tax credit for clean-energy factories.

Since then, solar equipment makers have announced nearly $43 billion in investments supporting a projected 48,000 jobs, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association.

Domestic manufacturing is now aligned with U.S. demand for solar panels, eliminating the need for panel imports. But that could change if a significant portion of U.S. factories caught up in the regulatory uncertainty are unable to compete.

The Trump-backed legislation restricts Chinese companies to 25% ownership stakes in plants seeking federal subsidies, imposes sourcing requirements, and prohibits effective control by Chinese firms. Companies say the subsidies, which include tax credits for solar manufacturing and installation, are crucial to remaining competitive.

Chinese companies have sought to comply by selling off factory stakes or otherwise restructuring. But most have preserved financial links to their U.S. plants, sometimes in the form of profit-sharing or supply deals, according to a Reuters review of corporate disclosures.

Industry officials have questions about whether those remaining links disqualify the factories from U.S. clean energy manufacturing credits. Absent guidance from the Treasury Department, installers including industry behemoth Sunrun are shunning these factories, while banks and insurers are withholding financing and coverage.

PARING BACK APPROVED SUPPLIERS

Sunrun in January circulated a pared-down list of approved solar-panel suppliers to installation partners, according to a document seen by Reuters.

The list included only non-Chinese manufacturers such as Qcells, REC, Silfab and Elin. Previously, it had included Canadian Solar, JA Solar, Jinko, LONGi and Trina - all of which are China-linked.

We have taken a conservative stance and do not procure equipment from manufacturers that would raise compliance concerns, Sunrun Deputy Chief Financial Officer Patrick Jobin said in a statement to Reuters.

Palmetto, a North Carolina-based company that sells rooftop solar panels, is also steering clear of China-linked producers despite their attempts at compliance, general manager Sean Hayes said.

Meanwhile, banks including Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan and Goldman Sachs have scaled back tax-equity financing for some solar projects due to concerns that future Treasury interpretations could retroactively invalidate tax credits, according to three people familiar with the deals who spoke on condition of anonymity.

The banks declined to comment.

Insurers have taken a harder line, refusing to insure companies against the risk they will be barred from clean-energy tax credits, according to Antony Joyce, a tax-insurance specialist at broker Marsh.

The companies that are best positioned right now are certainly the ones that didn't have clear ownership ties to a country of concern, said Peter Henderson, a principal at accounting firm Baker Tilly, who said Treasury's expected guidance will be crucial.

The Solar Energy Manufacturers for America Coalition, a trade group representing non-Chinese companies with U.S. factories, including First Solar and Hanwha's Qcells , has urged the Treasury Department to take a tough stance.

'CLEAREST WAY TO COMPLY'

The core issue driving firms away is that Chinese companies are maintaining ties with their factories instead of making a clean break. Factories that were originally built and operated by China-linked producers account for at least 25 gigawatts of the nation's about 66 GW of operating solar module manufacturing capacity.

Very few Chinese manufacturers are actually decoupling themselves from their U.S. factories entirely, said Elissa Pierce, an analyst at Wood Mackenzie.

China's JinkoSolar, which operates a factory in Florida, and the Chinese parent company of Boviet Solar, which produces panels in North Carolina, have said they are looking for outside investors.

Illuminate USA, a joint venture between China's LONGi and Chicago-based Invenergy, reduced the Chinese firm's ownership stake in an Ohio plant built in 2024 to below 25% and renegotiated its intellectual property agreement with LONGi, according to an Invenergy source.

But Invenergy is unsure if the plant, which employs around 1,700 workers, will survive. Illuminate and LONGi did not comment.

In March comments to the Internal Revenue Service urging clear guidance, the company said: The continued operation of Illuminate USA and other U.S. manufacturers remains at risk.

Source: DD News

urn:contentItem:6JJ1-J493-RRW2-B2DW-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Freeport- McMoRan EPS soars 118% Corporate Wire Date: 24 April 2026 09:28 EDT Release Date: April 23, 20262026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Freeport- McMoRan EPS soars 118% Corporate Wire Date: 24 April 2026 09:28 EDT Release Date: April 23, 2026

AMERICAN RESULTS

Freeport- McMoRan (NYSE:FCX), announced EPS of 61.0c for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 118% sequentially from 28.0c in the previous quarter [Q4/2025].

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 150.3% from $352.0m to $881.0m

- Total revenue was up 8.8% from $5.7b to $6.2b

- EPS growth 154.2% from 24.0c to 61.0c

- EBIT to Revenues was up from 22.5% to 32.6%

- Profit before tax to Revenues was up from 22.5% to 32.6%

- Profit after tax to Revenues was up from 6.1% to 14.1%

Quarterly Report (Quarter ending 31 Mar 2026)

Compared with the Previous Corresponding Period [PCP; Q1/2025], year-over-year [y.o.y.] EPS was up 154.2%, Revenue was up 8.8% and Net Profit was up 150.3%.

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Mar 2025EPS, c6124.0PCP growth in EPS %154-27Revenue, $ billion6.25.7PCP growth in Revenue %8.8-9.4Net Profit, $ million881352PCP growth in Net Profit %150-25.6

ACTIVITIES:

Freeport-McMoRan Inc. is a leading U.S.-based mining company specializing in the exploration, development, and production of copper, gold, and molybdenum. Headquartered in Phoenix, Arizona, the company operates large-scale mines in the Americas and Indonesia, leveraging advanced technologies to extract and process these essential metals for global industries such as electronics, construction, and renewable energy. Its core business focuses on sustainable resource management, emphasizing operational efficiency and environmental stewardship to meet rising demand. The fastest-growing segments include copper production, which has expanded rapidly due to increasing needs in electric vehicles and infrastructure projects, as well as gold operations that have benefited from higher prices and new mine developments. Overall, Freeport-McMoRan's strategic investments in high-potential assets position it as a key player in the global metals market, driving long-term value through diversified revenue streams and innovation.

MARKET POSITION:

Freeport- McMoRan Inc. (NYSE:FCX), the NYSE's 2nd largest Non Ferrous Metals sector company by market cap, soared $26.49 (or 75.7%) in the trailing 12 months to April 23, 2026 to close at $61.48. This means the stock has outperformed 84% of NYSE-listed stocks in the same period. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 34.4% over the same period, this is a relative price increase of 41.3%. Average daily volume of 15 million shares in the trailing year was 1.1 times average daily volume in the previous year.

urn:contentItem:6JJ5-CT13-RRP4-V3NW-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Freeport- McMoRan reports 117% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:37:10ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Freeport- McMoRan reports 117% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit

AMERICAN RESULTS

Freeport- McMoRan (NYSE:FCX), announced net profit of $881m for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 117% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and up 150% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025].

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 150.3% from $352.0m to $881.0m

- Total revenue was up 8.8% from $5.7b to $6.2b

- EPS growth 154.2% from 24.0c to 61.0c

- EBIT to Revenues was up from 22.5% to 32.6%

- Profit before tax to Revenues was up from 22.5% to 32.6%

- Profit after tax to Revenues was up from 6.1% to 14.1%

Quarterly Report (Quarter ending 31 Mar 2026)

Compared with the Previous Corresponding Period [PCP; Q1/2025], year-over-year [y.o.y.] EPS was up 154.2%, Revenue was up 8.8% and Net Profit was up 150.3%.

ACTIVITIES:

Freeport-McMoRan Inc. is a leading U.S.-based mining company specializing in the exploration, development, and production of copper, gold, and molybdenum. Headquartered in Phoenix, Arizona, the company operates large-scale mines in the Americas and Indonesia, leveraging advanced technologies to extract and process these essential metals for global industries such as electronics, construction, and renewable energy. Its core business focuses on sustainable resource management, emphasizing operational efficiency and environmental stewardship to meet rising demand. The fastest-growing segments include copper production, which has expanded rapidly due to increasing needs in electric vehicles and infrastructure projects, as well as gold operations that have benefited from higher prices and new mine developments. Overall, Freeport-McMoRan's strategic investments in high-potential assets position it as a key player in the global metals market, driving long-term value through diversified revenue streams and innovation.

MARKET POSITION:

Freeport- McMoRan Inc. (NYSE:FCX), the NYSE's 2nd largest Non Ferrous Metals sector company by market cap, soared $26.49 (or 75.7%) in the trailing 12 months to April 23, 2026 to close at $61.48. This means the stock has outperformed 84% of NYSE-listed stocks in the same period. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 34.4% over the same period, this is a relative price increase of 41.3%. Average daily volume of 15 million shares in the trailing year was 1.1 times average daily volume in the previous year.

SECTION 2 CORPORATE PROFILE

2.1 Activities

Pinnacle West Capital Corporation, a Phoenix, Arizona-based holding company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, primarily operates through its subsidiary Arizona Public Service Company (APS), which generates, transmits, and distributes electricity to millions of customers in Arizona. The company's core business focuses on providing reliable energy solutions, including a mix of coal, nuclear, natural gas, and renewable sources, serving residential, commercial, and industrial sectors. In recent years, the fastest-growing segments have been renewable energy initiatives, particularly solar power, driven by increasing demand for sustainable options, regulatory incentives, and technological advancements. This strategic emphasis on clean energy has bolstered Pinnacle West's portfolio, enhancing operational efficiency and long-term growth potential in the competitive utility market, while maintaining a commitment to environmental stewardship and grid reliability. It is the NYSE's 14th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

November 03 2025: Pinnacle West Reports 2025 Third-Quarter Financial Results

Customer growth, summer heat drive increase in sales and usage

Operating performance, customer reliability remain strong

Company expands support for community and utility bill assistance programs

PHOENIX-- Pinnacle West Capital Corp. (NYSE: PNW) today reported consolidated net income attributable to common shareholders for the 2025 third quarter of $413.2 million, or $3.39 per diluted share of common stock. This result compares with consolidated net income of $395.0 million, or $3.37 per share of common stock, for the same period a year ago. However, through the first three quarters of the current year, the company's earnings were 2.4% lower than in the first nine months of 2024.

The 2025 third-quarter results reflect an increase of about $18 million compared to the 2024 third quarter, primarily as a result of increased customer usage, customer growth and related pricing; higher transmission service revenues; lower operations and maintenance expenses; higher AFUDC; lower depreciation and amortization expenses mostly due to operations ceasing at Cholla, partially offset by increased plant additions and intangible assets. These positive factors were partially offset by the unfavorable impacts of weather effects versus the same period a year ago; higher interest charges; lower pension and other post-retirement non-service credits; higher income taxes due to higher pre-tax income and lower tax credits.

"Driven by one of the fastest-growing service territories in the country and the third-hottest Arizona summer on record, we experienced an increase in retail sales that helped contribute to solid third-quarter financial results," said Pinnacle West Chairman, President and Chief Executive Officer Ted Geisler. "In addition, our employees continued to do what they do best: maintaining reliable electric service for our 1.4 million customers.
